options: An Rh-positive mother carries an Rh-negative fetus
An Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus
Both mother and fetus are Rh-negative
s2.explain: Rh incompatibility occurs when an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus, and maternal exposure to Rh-positive RBCs causes sensitization.

s2.q: Fetal red blood cells enter the maternal circulation mainly at:
s2.explain: Fetomaternal hemorrhage occurs mainly at delivery when the placenta separates, or during invasive procedures/abdominal trauma.

s2.q: Maternal anti-D antibodies are of which immunoglobulin class and cross the placenta?
s2.explain: IgG antibodies cross the placenta and cause fetal hemolysis. Initial sensitization produces IgM, but subsequent exposure produces IgG.

s2.q: Exchange transfusion in the Rh-affected neonate is performed to:
options: Remove bilirubin and anti-D antibodies, and correct anemia
s2.explain: Exchange transfusion removes bilirubin, anti-D antibodies, and sensitized RBCs while correcting anemia with compatible Rh-negative blood.

s2.q: ABO incompatibility differs from Rh incompatibility in that:
options: ABO is more severe and often causes hydrops
ABO often affects the first pregnancy and is typically milder
ABO only affects mothers with blood type AB
s2.explain: ABO incompatibility often affects the first pregnancy (mild) as anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring IgM that don't cross placenta well. It is typically milder than Rh disease.

s3.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s3.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s3.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s3.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.
